lesson.subtitle: Aula 6 -- Handling Pushback -- Disagreeing Professionally
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Rafael Pelizaro | Aula 6 -- Handling Pushback

listenings.text: Hi, this is Rafael. I am recording a quick note about the planning call this afternoon. The product owner wants to add a new login feature to the current sprint. I understand the idea, but I have a concern about the deadline. If we add this feature now, we will miss the milestone on Friday. There is also a trade-off with quality, because the team will not have time to test it well. My objection is not the feature itself. My objection is the timing. So in the call I will see her point first, then I will explain the constraint, and then I will propose a compromise. If we move the feature to the next sprint, we will protect the deadline and still deliver it. I want us to align on the plan before we talk to the client. I will keep my tone calm and professional, and I will not just say no. I will explain my rationale and offer a clear option.

Hi Rafael, this is Sandra. Thank you for your note about the new feature. I see your point about the deadline, and I share your concern. You are right that if we add it now, we will probably miss Friday. I do not want to break the milestone either. Here is my assumption, and please correct me if I am wrong: the client mainly wants the feature before the end of the month, not this exact week. If that is true, your compromise works for me. Let us move the login feature to the next sprint, protect the deadline, and tell the client the new date with a clear rationale. I think that is a fair trade-off. Could you prepare a short summary of the decision, so we both align on it before the client call? If you send it today, I will review it tonight. Thank you for raising this early and so professionally.

extra_audio.text: First, acknowledge the other person and say I see your point. Next, state your concern clearly and calmly. Then explain the consequence with a first conditional, for example if we do that, we will miss the deadline. After that, propose a compromise or a trade-off instead of just saying no. Finally, confirm the next step and keep a positive, professional tone.
